name: Layered (N-Tier) Architecture
summary: Separates system concerns into presentation, business, and data access layers.
best_for: Enterprise CRUD systems with clear separation of concerns and stable domain rules.
tradeoffs: Can become rigid and chatty across layers; performance may degrade with deep call chains.
constraints: Works best with well-defined boundaries and disciplined dependency direction.
anti_patterns: Avoid for ultra-low latency systems requiring tight data-to-UI paths.

name: Microservices Architecture
summary: Builds an application as independently deployable services aligned to business capabilities.
best_for: Large, evolving products with multiple teams needing autonomous delivery.
tradeoffs: Operational complexity, distributed debugging, and higher platform overhead.
constraints: Needs strong DevOps, observability, API governance, and service ownership.
anti_patterns: Avoid for small teams or early products without platform maturity.

name: Modular Monolith
summary: Single deployable unit organized into strict internal modules with clear interfaces.
best_for: Startups or mid-size teams needing fast delivery without distributed-system overhead.
tradeoffs: Requires discipline to prevent module coupling; eventual split can be hard if boundaries are weak.
constraints: Enforce module boundaries in code and ownership conventions.
anti_patterns: Avoid when teams are fully independent and require isolated release cycles.

name: Event-Driven Architecture
summary: Components communicate via events using brokers or streams for loose coupling.
best_for: Real-time workflows, integrations, and systems that react to business events.
tradeoffs: Event ordering, replay, and eventual consistency increase complexity.
constraints: Requires schema governance, idempotency, and robust monitoring.
anti_patterns: Avoid when strict synchronous consistency is mandatory everywhere.

name: CQRS
summary: Separates read and write models to optimize each path independently.
best_for: Domains with complex writes and high-volume, query-heavy read traffic.
tradeoffs: Higher complexity, data duplication, and consistency lag between models.
constraints: Needs careful command validation and read-model projection strategy.
anti_patterns: Avoid for simple CRUD workloads with modest scale.

name: Event Sourcing
summary: Persists state changes as an immutable sequence of domain events.
best_for: Audit-heavy domains requiring traceability and historical reconstruction.
tradeoffs: Complex debugging, migration burden, and projection management.
constraints: Requires versioned events, replay strategy, and snapshotting plan.
anti_patterns: Avoid when full audit history is not a strong business need.

name: Serverless Architecture
summary: Runs business logic on managed functions and services with auto-scaling.
best_for: Spiky workloads, rapid experimentation, and event-triggered tasks.
tradeoffs: Cold starts, vendor lock-in, and local debugging challenges.
constraints: Design around stateless functions, managed services, and cloud limits.
anti_patterns: Avoid for long-running, compute-heavy, or strict low-latency workloads.

name: Hexagonal (Ports and Adapters)
summary: Centers business logic and isolates external systems through ports and adapters.
best_for: Domain-centric systems needing testability and flexibility in infrastructure choices.
tradeoffs: Initial setup feels abstract and may slow inexperienced teams.
constraints: Requires clear port contracts and disciplined dependency inversion.
anti_patterns: Avoid for short-lived prototypes where design overhead is not justified.

name: Clean Architecture
summary: Organizes code in concentric layers with dependencies pointing inward to core rules.
best_for: Long-lived applications prioritizing maintainability, testing, and framework independence.
tradeoffs: More boilerplate and abstraction compared to direct framework-driven approaches.
constraints: Strong code review discipline is needed to protect dependency rules.
anti_patterns: Avoid for throwaway demos with minimal lifecycle expectations.

name: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) Bounded Contexts
summary: Structures systems around domain language and distinct bounded contexts.
best_for: Complex business domains with many subdomains and evolving rules.
tradeoffs: Discovery cost is high; poor facilitation leads to overengineering.
constraints: Needs domain experts and deliberate context mapping.
anti_patterns: Avoid for simple transactional systems with limited domain complexity.

name: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)
summary: Integrates enterprise capabilities via reusable services and contracts.
best_for: Large enterprises integrating heterogeneous legacy and vendor systems.
tradeoffs: Governance-heavy and slower change cycles if central coordination is strict.
constraints: Requires mature contract governance and enterprise integration standards.
anti_patterns: Avoid for small greenfield products where simpler approaches suffice.

name: Client-Server Architecture
summary: Separates clients consuming services from centralized backend servers.
best_for: Traditional web and desktop systems with centralized control.
tradeoffs: Server bottlenecks can appear without proper horizontal scaling.
constraints: Needs robust session, caching, and load-balancing strategy.
anti_patterns: Avoid for offline-first products requiring strong local autonomy.

name: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture
summary: Distributes roles among nodes that can act as both clients and servers.
best_for: Decentralized file sharing, collaboration, and resilient distributed workloads.
tradeoffs: Security, trust, and coordination are harder than centralized designs.
constraints: Requires robust peer discovery, trust model, and fault tolerance.
anti_patterns: Avoid when strict central governance and compliance controls are required.

name: Pipeline Architecture
summary: Processes data through ordered stages where each step transforms input for the next.
best_for: ETL, media processing, and document transformation workflows.
tradeoffs: Backpressure handling and stage imbalance can reduce throughput.
constraints: Needs clear contracts between stages and retry semantics.
anti_patterns: Avoid for highly interactive request-response applications.

name: Space-Based Architecture
summary: Uses distributed in-memory data grids to remove central database bottlenecks.
best_for: High-throughput, low-latency systems with bursty traffic.
tradeoffs: Operational complexity and data consistency strategy are non-trivial.
constraints: Requires expertise in partitioning, replication, and failure recovery.
anti_patterns: Avoid for standard enterprise apps with moderate load.

name: Master-Slave Architecture
summary: Coordinates work through one controller node and replicated worker nodes.
best_for: Read-heavy systems, batch control, and replicated processing tasks.
tradeoffs: Single point of coordination risk unless failover is strong.
constraints: Needs clear failover and replication lag management.
anti_patterns: Avoid for fully decentralized requirements.

name: Broker Architecture
summary: Uses a broker to mediate communication and routing between distributed components.
best_for: Integration-heavy systems where producers and consumers evolve independently.
tradeoffs: Broker can become bottleneck or central failure domain if underprovisioned.
constraints: Requires queue/topic governance, dead-letter strategy, and monitoring.
anti_patterns: Avoid for simple direct service interactions.

name: Blackboard Architecture
summary: Multiple specialized components collaborate by reading/writing to a shared knowledge base.
best_for: Complex problem-solving systems like signal interpretation or AI planning.
tradeoffs: Coordination logic is hard and performance can degrade with contention.
constraints: Needs strong conflict resolution and contribution arbitration rules.
anti_patterns: Avoid for transactional business apps with straightforward flows.

name: Microkernel (Plug-in) Architecture
summary: Provides a minimal core with optional plug-in modules for extensibility.
best_for: Products requiring customizable features and plugin ecosystems.
tradeoffs: Plugin contracts and version compatibility create long-term maintenance overhead.
constraints: Requires stable extension APIs and strict plugin isolation.
anti_patterns: Avoid when product scope is fixed and extension is unnecessary.

name: Strangler Fig Pattern
summary: Incrementally replaces legacy functionality by routing traffic to new components over time.
best_for: Legacy modernization with low-risk, phased migration needs.
tradeoffs: Dual-run complexity and temporary integration overhead are significant.
constraints: Needs clear migration roadmap, traffic routing, and parity testing.
anti_patterns: Avoid when full rewrite is cheap and low risk.

name: Backend for Frontend (BFF)
summary: Creates dedicated backend services tailored to each frontend channel.
best_for: Products with distinct web/mobile clients needing optimized APIs.
tradeoffs: Can duplicate logic across BFFs without shared domain services.
constraints: Needs clear ownership split between channel logic and core services.
anti_patterns: Avoid when one generic API serves all clients well.

name: API Gateway Pattern
summary: Central entry point handling routing, auth, throttling, and cross-cutting concerns.
best_for: Service-based backends needing unified external API management.
tradeoffs: Gateway complexity can grow and become critical dependency.
constraints: Requires policy governance, observability, and resilience design.
anti_patterns: Avoid if there are very few services and no cross-cutting needs.

name: Data Mesh
summary: Decentralizes data ownership to domain teams with shared platform standards.
best_for: Large organizations with many data domains and platform capabilities.
tradeoffs: Requires significant organizational maturity and governance alignment.
constraints: Needs federated governance, self-serve platform, and data product mindset.
anti_patterns: Avoid for small teams with one central data platform.

name: Lakehouse Architecture
summary: Combines data lake flexibility with warehouse-like management and analytics.
best_for: Analytics and ML workloads requiring unified storage for varied data types.
tradeoffs: Tooling selection and table-format governance can be complex.
constraints: Requires metadata management, data quality controls, and cost governance.
anti_patterns: Avoid for applications with minimal reporting and no ML roadmap.

name: Offline-First Architecture
summary: Prioritizes local-first UX with sync mechanisms for intermittent connectivity.
best_for: Mobile or field applications used in low-connectivity environments.
tradeoffs: Conflict resolution and sync reconciliation add substantial complexity.
constraints: Needs robust local storage model and deterministic sync rules.
anti_patterns: Avoid when users are always online and consistency is immediate.
